The Kibi clan features in [[:en:Age of Empires (video game)|Age of Empires]] as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412645]]
Wikidata name: Kibi clan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412645]]
Wikidata name: Kibi clan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|%E5%90%89%E5%82%99%E6%B0%8F|date=March 2024}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|home province=[[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]]}}

The '''Kibi clan''' was a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]<ref>https://www.city.okayama.jp/shisei/cmsfiles/contents/0000012/12189/000196899.pdf</ref> centered in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YCQdAQAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Korean Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean Social Science Research Council |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gKbrAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean National Commission for Unesco. |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a">{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gBgVAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Japan Quarterly |date=1979 |publisher=Asahi Shinbun |language=en}}</ref> descended from {{ill|Wakatakehiko|ja|%E7%A8%9A%E6%AD%A6%E5%BD%A6%E5%91%BD|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakatakehiko|qq=}} the son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]].

[[Kibi no Makibi]], the founder of [[Hiromine Shrine]] was a famous member.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref>

They had navigational authority over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a" />

They had a prominent Iron manufacturing apparatus.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fxEtAQAAIAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=The East |date=1982 |publisher=East Publications |language=en}}</ref>

They alternatively may descend from [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] who roughly ruled [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fiJTyA6FsMsC&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA12 |title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context |date=1995 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-873410-42-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AYX7AQAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PP15 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24705-9 |language=en}}</ref>

[[Kibidera]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]] is the clan temple of the clan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCallum |first=Donald F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA100 |title=The Four Great Temples: Buddhist Archaeology, Architecture, and Icons of Seventh-Century Japan |date=2008-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6473-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Association with Kibitsu Shrine ==
The [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine's]] rites are closely to the clan. The shrine's gods are the Kibi clan's ancestors who have become gods. These ancestors protect the Kibi area.<ref name="hardacre-2023a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nunQEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA239 |title=Marketing the Menacing Fetus in Japan |date=2023-09-01 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-92204-4 |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine started as a place for the Kibi clan. It has the clan's ancestors as gods. This gives the shrine a pure and protective feel. The ancestors are seen as good and helpful spirits. This is how the shrine connects with [[:en:Mizuko kuyō|mizuko]]. [[:en:Mizuko kuyō|Mizuko]] means the souls of babies who died early or were not born.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine places the [[:en:Mizuko kuyō|mizuko]] shrine next to the ancestor shrine. It also uses running water. This setup has several meanings:<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

# The water purifies the souls of [[:en:Mizuko kuyō|mizuko]]. [[:en:Mizuko kuyō|Mizuko]] is linked to water.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# Being near the ancestor shrine suggests these souls become kind and protective spirits.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# As protective spirits, the [[:en:Mizuko kuyō|mizuko]] guard those who remember them.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

This way, the Kibitsu Shrine uses its ties to the Kibi clan. It creates a respectful place for [[:en:Mizuko kuyō|mizuko]]. It links them to the shrine's ancestral and protective gods.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

== Harima Fudoki ==
The story of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and Inami-no-Wakiiratsume in the [[Harima Fudoki]] shows the relationship between the Kibi clan and the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. It suggests that the [[:en:Inami, Hyōgo|Inami]] area was once a border between the Yamato and Kibi regions.<ref name="palmer-2015a">{{Cite book |last=Palmer |first=Edwina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWzsCgAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA71 |title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated, and with Commentary |date=2015-11-09 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-26937-8 |language=en}}</ref>

Inami-no-Wakiiratsume, a woman from Inami, is mentioned in several ancient texts. She is thought to be the younger sister of Oiratsume from the "Kojiki,". She might have been a queen in Harima.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

The Harima Fudoki does not mention Otarashihiko/Keiko as an emperor. Instead, Otarashihiko might have been a local leader in the Inami district of the Harima coast.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

== Kibi clan rebellion ==
{{Main|Kibi Clan Rebellion}}
The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato state]] on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] had moved him to the Japanese post at [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's [[:en:Protohistory|proto-historic]] period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of [[:en:Mimasaka Province|Mimasaka Province]]. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial government]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in [[:en:Age of Empires (video game)|Age of Empires]] as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412645]]
Wikidata name: Kibi clan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412645]]
Wikidata name: Kibi clan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|%E5%90%89%E5%82%99%E6%B0%8F|date=March 2024}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|home province={{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}}}}

The '''Kibi clan''' was a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]<ref>https://www.city.okayama.jp/shisei/cmsfiles/contents/0000012/12189/000196899.pdf</ref> centered in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YCQdAQAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Korean Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean Social Science Research Council |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gKbrAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean National Commission for Unesco. |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a">{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gBgVAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Japan Quarterly |date=1979 |publisher=Asahi Shinbun |language=en}}</ref> descended from {{ill|Wakatakehiko|ja|%E7%A8%9A%E6%AD%A6%E5%BD%A6%E5%91%BD|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakatakehiko|qq=}} the son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}}.

[[Kibi no Makibi]], the founder of [[Hiromine Shrine]] was a famous member.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref>

They had navigational authority over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a" />

They had a prominent Iron manufacturing apparatus.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fxEtAQAAIAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=The East |date=1982 |publisher=East Publications |language=en}}</ref>

They alternatively may descend from [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] who roughly ruled {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fiJTyA6FsMsC&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA12 |title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context |date=1995 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-873410-42-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AYX7AQAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PP15 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24705-9 |language=en}}</ref>

[[Kibidera]] in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai}} is the clan temple of the clan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCallum |first=Donald F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA100 |title=The Four Great Temples: Buddhist Archaeology, Architecture, and Icons of Seventh-Century Japan |date=2008-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6473-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Association with Kibitsu Shrine ==
The [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine's]] rites are closely to the clan. The shrine's gods are the Kibi clan's ancestors who have become gods. These ancestors protect the Kibi area.<ref name="hardacre-2023a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nunQEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA239 |title=Marketing the Menacing Fetus in Japan |date=2023-09-01 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-92204-4 |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine started as a place for the Kibi clan. It has the clan's ancestors as gods. This gives the shrine a pure and protective feel. The ancestors are seen as good and helpful spirits. This is how the shrine connects with {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} means the souls of babies who died early or were not born.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine places the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} shrine next to the ancestor shrine. It also uses running water. This setup has several meanings:<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

# The water purifies the souls of {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} is linked to water.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# Being near the ancestor shrine suggests these souls become kind and protective spirits.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# As protective spirits, the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} guard those who remember them.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

This way, the Kibitsu Shrine uses its ties to the Kibi clan. It creates a respectful place for {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. It links them to the shrine's ancestral and protective gods.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

== Harima Fudoki ==
The story of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and Inami-no-Wakiiratsume in the [[Harima Fudoki]] shows the relationship between the Kibi clan and the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}. It suggests that the {{ill|Inami, Hyōgo|en|Inami, Hyōgo|WD=Q1155194|lt=Inami}} area was once a border between the Yamato and Kibi regions.<ref name="palmer-2015a">{{Cite book |last=Palmer |first=Edwina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWzsCgAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA71 |title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated, and with Commentary |date=2015-11-09 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-26937-8 |language=en}}</ref>

Inami-no-Wakiiratsume, a woman from Inami, is mentioned in several ancient texts. She is thought to be the younger sister of Oiratsume from the "Kojiki,". She might have been a queen in Harima.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

The Harima Fudoki does not mention Otarashihiko/Keiko as an emperor. Instead, Otarashihiko might have been a local leader in the Inami district of the Harima coast.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

== Kibi clan rebellion ==
{{Main|Kibi Clan Rebellion}}
The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province}} were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial government}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|WD=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Genealogy ==
{{Generations of Jimmu}}

== See also ==

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
* {{cite book|last1=Brinkley|first1=FRank|author-link=Francis Brinkley|title=A history of the Japanese people: From the earliest times to the end of the Meiji era|url=https://archive.org/details/ahistoryjapanes01kikugoog|year=1915|publisher=[[Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.|Encyclopædia Britannica Co]]}}

* {{cite book|last1=Brinkley|first1=FRank|author-link=Francis Brinkley|title=A history of the Japanese people: From the earliest times to the end of the Meiji era|url=https://archive.org/details/ahistoryjapanes01kikugoog|year=1915|publisher=[[Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.|Encyclopædia Britannica Co]]}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412645]]
Wikidata name: Kibi clan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412645]]
Wikidata name: Kibi clan
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|%E5%90%89%E5%82%99%E6%B0%8F|date=March 2024}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|home province={{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}}}}

The '''Kibi clan''' was a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]<ref>https://www.city.okayama.jp/shisei/cmsfiles/contents/0000012/12189/000196899.pdf</ref> centered in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YCQdAQAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Korean Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean Social Science Research Council |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gKbrAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean National Commission for Unesco. |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a">{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gBgVAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Japan Quarterly |date=1979 |publisher=Asahi Shinbun |language=en}}</ref> descended from {{ill|Wakatakehiko|ja|%E7%A8%9A%E6%AD%A6%E5%BD%A6%E5%91%BD|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakatakehiko|qq=}} the son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}}.

[[Kibi no Makibi]], the founder of [[Hiromine Shrine]] was a famous member.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref>

They had navigational authority over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a" />

They had a prominent Iron manufacturing apparatus.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fxEtAQAAIAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=The East |date=1982 |publisher=East Publications |language=en}}</ref>

They alternatively may descend from [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] who roughly ruled {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fiJTyA6FsMsC&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA12 |title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context |date=1995 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-873410-42-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AYX7AQAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PP15 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24705-9 |language=en}}</ref>

[[Kibidera]] in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai}} is the clan temple of the clan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCallum |first=Donald F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA100 |title=The Four Great Temples: Buddhist Archaeology, Architecture, and Icons of Seventh-Century Japan |date=2008-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6473-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Association with Kibitsu Shrine ==
The [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine's]] rites are closely to the clan. The shrine's gods are the Kibi clan's ancestors who have become gods. These ancestors protect the Kibi area.<ref name="hardacre-2023a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nunQEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA239 |title=Marketing the Menacing Fetus in Japan |date=2023-09-01 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-92204-4 |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine started as a place for the Kibi clan. It has the clan's ancestors as gods. This gives the shrine a pure and protective feel. The ancestors are seen as good and helpful spirits. This is how the shrine connects with {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} means the souls of babies who died early or were not born.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine places the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} shrine next to the ancestor shrine. It also uses running water. This setup has several meanings:<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

# The water purifies the souls of {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} is linked to water.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# Being near the ancestor shrine suggests these souls become kind and protective spirits.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# As protective spirits, the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} guard those who remember them.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

This way, the Kibitsu Shrine uses its ties to the Kibi clan. It creates a respectful place for {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. It links them to the shrine's ancestral and protective gods.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

== Harima Fudoki ==
The story of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and Inami-no-Wakiiratsume in the [[Harima Fudoki]] shows the relationship between the Kibi clan and the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}. It suggests that the {{ill|Inami, Hyōgo|en|Inami, Hyōgo|WD=Q1155194|lt=Inami}} area was once a border between the Yamato and Kibi regions.<ref name="palmer-2015a">{{Cite book |last=Palmer |first=Edwina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWzsCgAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA71 |title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated, and with Commentary |date=2015-11-09 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-26937-8 |language=en}}</ref>

Inami-no-Wakiiratsume, a woman from Inami, is mentioned in several ancient texts. She is thought to be the younger sister of Oiratsume from the "Kojiki,". She might have been a queen in Harima.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

The Harima Fudoki does not mention Otarashihiko/Keiko as an emperor. Instead, Otarashihiko might have been a local leader in the Inami district of the Harima coast.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

== Kibi clan rebellion ==
{{Main|Kibi Clan Rebellion}}
The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province}} were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial government}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|WD=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Genealogy ==

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]


{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|%E5%90%89%E5%82%99%E6%B0%8F|date=March 2024}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|home province={{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}}}}

The '''Kibi clan''' was a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]<ref>https://www.city.okayama.jp/shisei/cmsfiles/contents/0000012/12189/000196899.pdf</ref> centered in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YCQdAQAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Korean Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean Social Science Research Council |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gKbrAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean National Commission for Unesco. |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a">{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gBgVAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Japan Quarterly |date=1979 |publisher=Asahi Shinbun |language=en}}</ref> descended from {{ill|Wakatakehiko|ja|%E7%A8%9A%E6%AD%A6%E5%BD%A6%E5%91%BD|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakatakehiko|qq=}} the son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}}.

[[Kibi no Makibi]], the founder of [[Hiromine Shrine]] was a famous member.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref>

They had navigational authority over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a" />

They had a prominent Iron manufacturing apparatus.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fxEtAQAAIAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=The East |date=1982 |publisher=East Publications |language=en}}</ref>

They alternatively may descend from [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] who roughly ruled {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fiJTyA6FsMsC&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA12 |title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context |date=1995 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-873410-42-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AYX7AQAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PP15 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24705-9 |language=en}}</ref>

[[Kibidera]] in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai}} is the clan temple of the clan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCallum |first=Donald F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA100 |title=The Four Great Temples: Buddhist Archaeology, Architecture, and Icons of Seventh-Century Japan |date=2008-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6473-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Association with Kibitsu Shrine ==
The [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine's]] rites are closely to the clan. The shrine's gods are the Kibi clan's ancestors who have become gods. These ancestors protect the Kibi area.<ref name="hardacre-2023a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nunQEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA239 |title=Marketing the Menacing Fetus in Japan |date=2023-09-01 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-92204-4 |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine started as a place for the Kibi clan. It has the clan's ancestors as gods. This gives the shrine a pure and protective feel. The ancestors are seen as good and helpful spirits. This is how the shrine connects with {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} means the souls of babies who died early or were not born.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine places the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} shrine next to the ancestor shrine. It also uses running water. This setup has several meanings:<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

# The water purifies the souls of {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} is linked to water.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# Being near the ancestor shrine suggests these souls become kind and protective spirits.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# As protective spirits, the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} guard those who remember them.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

This way, the Kibitsu Shrine uses its ties to the Kibi clan. It creates a respectful place for {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. It links them to the shrine's ancestral and protective gods.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

== Harima Fudoki ==
The story of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and Inami-no-Wakiiratsume in the [[Harima Fudoki]] shows the relationship between the Kibi clan and the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}. It suggests that the {{ill|Inami, Hyōgo|en|Inami, Hyōgo|WD=Q1155194|lt=Inami}} area was once a border between the Yamato and Kibi regions.<ref name="palmer-2015a">{{Cite book |last=Palmer |first=Edwina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWzsCgAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA71 |title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated, and with Commentary |date=2015-11-09 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-26937-8 |language=en}}</ref>

Inami-no-Wakiiratsume, a woman from Inami, is mentioned in several ancient texts. She is thought to be the younger sister of Oiratsume from the "Kojiki,". She might have been a queen in Harima.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

The Harima Fudoki does not mention Otarashihiko/Keiko as an emperor. Instead, Otarashihiko might have been a local leader in the Inami district of the Harima coast.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

== Kibi clan rebellion ==
{{Main|Kibi Clan Rebellion}}
The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province}} were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial government}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|WD=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]


{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|%E5%90%89%E5%82%99%E6%B0%8F|date=March 2024}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|home province={{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}}}}

The '''Kibi clan''' was a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]<ref>https://www.city.okayama.jp/shisei/cmsfiles/contents/0000012/12189/000196899.pdf</ref> centered in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YCQdAQAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Korean Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean Social Science Research Council |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gKbrAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean National Commission for Unesco. |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a">{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gBgVAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Japan Quarterly |date=1979 |publisher=Asahi Shinbun |language=en}}</ref> descended from {{ill|Wakatakehiko|ja|%E7%A8%9A%E6%AD%A6%E5%BD%A6%E5%91%BD|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakatakehiko|qq=}} the son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}}.

[[Kibi no Makibi]], the founder of [[Hiromine Shrine]] was a famous member.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref>

They had navigational authority over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a" />

They had a prominent Iron manufacturing apparatus.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fxEtAQAAIAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=The East |date=1982 |publisher=East Publications |language=en}}</ref>

They alternatively may descend from [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] who roughly ruled {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fiJTyA6FsMsC&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA12 |title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context |date=1995 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-873410-42-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AYX7AQAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PP15 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24705-9 |language=en}}</ref>

[[Kibidera]] in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai}} is the clan temple of the clan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCallum |first=Donald F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA100 |title=The Four Great Temples: Buddhist Archaeology, Architecture, and Icons of Seventh-Century Japan |date=2008-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6473-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Association with Kibitsu Shrine ==
The [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine's]] rites are closely to the clan. The shrine's gods are the Kibi clan's ancestors who have become gods. These ancestors protect the Kibi area.<ref name="hardacre-2023a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nunQEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA239 |title=Marketing the Menacing Fetus in Japan |date=2023-09-01 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-92204-4 |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine started as a place for the Kibi clan. It has the clan's ancestors as gods. This gives the shrine a pure and protective feel. The ancestors are seen as good and helpful spirits. This is how the shrine connects with {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} means the souls of babies who died early or were not born.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine places the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} shrine next to the ancestor shrine. It also uses running water. This setup has several meanings:<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

# The water purifies the souls of {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko}} is linked to water.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# Being near the ancestor shrine suggests these souls become kind and protective spirits.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# As protective spirits, the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}} guard those who remember them.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

This way, the Kibitsu Shrine uses its ties to the Kibi clan. It creates a respectful place for {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko}}. It links them to the shrine's ancestral and protective gods.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

== Harima Fudoki ==
The story of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and Inami-no-Wakiiratsume in the [[Harima Fudoki]] shows the relationship between the Kibi clan and the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}. It suggests that the {{ill|Inami, Hyōgo|en|Inami, Hyōgo|WD=Q1155194|lt=Inami}} area was once a border between the Yamato and Kibi regions.<ref name="palmer-2015a">{{Cite book |last=Palmer |first=Edwina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWzsCgAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA71 |title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated, and with Commentary |date=2015-11-09 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-26937-8 |language=en}}</ref>

Inami-no-Wakiiratsume, a woman from Inami, is mentioned in several ancient texts. She is thought to be the younger sister of Oiratsume from the "Kojiki,". She might have been a queen in Harima.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

The Harima Fudoki does not mention Otarashihiko/Keiko as an emperor. Instead, Otarashihiko might have been a local leader in the Inami district of the Harima coast.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

== Kibi clan rebellion ==
{{Main|Kibi Clan Rebellion}}
The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province}} were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial government}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|WD=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Genealogy ==
{{Generations of Jimmu}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]

{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|%E5%90%89%E5%82%99%E6%B0%8F|date=March 2024}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|home province={{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}}}}

The '''Kibi clan''' was a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]<ref>https://www.city.okayama.jp/shisei/cmsfiles/contents/0000012/12189/000196899.pdf</ref> centered in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YCQdAQAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Korean Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean Social Science Research Council |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gKbrAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean National Commission for Unesco. |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a">{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gBgVAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Japan Quarterly |date=1979 |publisher=Asahi Shinbun |language=en}}</ref> descended from {{ill|Wakatakehiko|ja|%E7%A8%9A%E6%AD%A6%E5%BD%A6%E5%91%BD|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakatakehiko|qq=}} the son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}}.

[[Kibi no Makibi]], the founder of [[Hiromine Shrine]] was a famous member.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref>

They had navigational authority over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a" />

They had a prominent Iron manufacturing apparatus.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fxEtAQAAIAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=The East |date=1982 |publisher=East Publications |language=en}}</ref>

They alternatively may descend from [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] who roughly ruled {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fiJTyA6FsMsC&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA12 |title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context |date=1995 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-873410-42-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AYX7AQAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PP15 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24705-9 |language=en}}</ref>

[[Kibidera]] in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai|1=Sakurai, Nara}} is the clan temple of the clan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCallum |first=Donald F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA100 |title=The Four Great Temples: Buddhist Archaeology, Architecture, and Icons of Seventh-Century Japan |date=2008-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6473-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Association with Kibitsu Shrine ==
The [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine's]] rites are closely to the clan. The shrine's gods are the Kibi clan's ancestors who have become gods. These ancestors protect the Kibi area.<ref name="hardacre-2023a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nunQEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA239 |title=Marketing the Menacing Fetus in Japan |date=2023-09-01 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-92204-4 |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine started as a place for the Kibi clan. It has the clan's ancestors as gods. This gives the shrine a pure and protective feel. The ancestors are seen as good and helpful spirits. This is how the shrine connects with {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} means the souls of babies who died early or were not born.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine places the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} shrine next to the ancestor shrine. It also uses running water. This setup has several meanings:<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

# The water purifies the souls of {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} is linked to water.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# Being near the ancestor shrine suggests these souls become kind and protective spirits.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# As protective spirits, the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} guard those who remember them.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

This way, the Kibitsu Shrine uses its ties to the Kibi clan. It creates a respectful place for {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}}. It links them to the shrine's ancestral and protective gods.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

== Harima Fudoki ==
The story of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}} and Inami-no-Wakiiratsume in the [[Harima Fudoki]] shows the relationship between the Kibi clan and the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}. It suggests that the {{ill|Inami, Hyōgo|en|Inami, Hyōgo|WD=Q1155194|lt=Inami|1=Inami, Hyōgo}} area was once a border between the Yamato and Kibi regions.<ref name="palmer-2015a">{{Cite book |last=Palmer |first=Edwina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWzsCgAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA71 |title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated, and with Commentary |date=2015-11-09 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-26937-8 |language=en}}</ref>

Inami-no-Wakiiratsume, a woman from Inami, is mentioned in several ancient texts. She is thought to be the younger sister of Oiratsume from the "Kojiki,". She might have been a queen in Harima.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

The Harima Fudoki does not mention Otarashihiko/Keiko as an emperor. Instead, Otarashihiko might have been a local leader in the Inami district of the Harima coast.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

== Kibi clan rebellion ==
{{Main|Kibi Clan Rebellion}}
The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic|1=Protohistory}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province|1=Mimasaka Province}}. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial government|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|WD=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires|1=Age of Empires (video game)}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial government|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|WD=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires|1=Age of Empires (video game)}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi clan]][[ja:吉備氏]][[zh:吉備氏]]


{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|%E5%90%89%E5%82%99%E6%B0%8F|date=March 2024}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|home province={{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}}}}

The '''Kibi clan''' was a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]<ref>https://www.city.okayama.jp/shisei/cmsfiles/contents/0000012/12189/000196899.pdf</ref> centered in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YCQdAQAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Korean Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean Social Science Research Council |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gKbrAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Social Science Journal |date=1983 |publisher=Korean National Commission for Unesco. |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a">{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gBgVAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Japan Quarterly |date=1979 |publisher=Asahi Shinbun |language=en}}</ref> descended from {{ill|Wakatakehiko|ja|%E7%A8%9A%E6%AD%A6%E5%BD%A6%E5%91%BD|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakatakehiko|qq=}} the son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}}.

[[Kibi no Makibi]], the founder of [[Hiromine Shrine]] was a famous member.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref>

They had navigational authority over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="asahi-shinbun-1979a" />

They had a prominent Iron manufacturing apparatus.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fxEtAQAAIAAJ&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=The East |date=1982 |publisher=East Publications |language=en}}</ref>

They alternatively may descend from [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] who roughly ruled {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fiJTyA6FsMsC&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA12 |title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context |date=1995 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-873410-42-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AYX7AQAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PP15 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24705-9 |language=en}}</ref>

[[Kibidera]] in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai|1=Sakurai, Nara}} is the clan temple of the clan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCallum |first=Donald F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA100 |title=The Four Great Temples: Buddhist Archaeology, Architecture, and Icons of Seventh-Century Japan |date=2008-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6473-6 |language=en}}</ref>

== Association with Kibitsu Shrine ==
The [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine's]] rites are closely to the clan. The shrine's gods are the Kibi clan's ancestors who have become gods. These ancestors protect the Kibi area.<ref name="hardacre-2023a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nunQEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA239 |title=Marketing the Menacing Fetus in Japan |date=2023-09-01 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-92204-4 |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine started as a place for the Kibi clan. It has the clan's ancestors as gods. This gives the shrine a pure and protective feel. The ancestors are seen as good and helpful spirits. This is how the shrine connects with {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} means the souls of babies who died early or were not born.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

The shrine places the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} shrine next to the ancestor shrine. It also uses running water. This setup has several meanings:<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

# The water purifies the souls of {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}}. {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} is linked to water.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# Being near the ancestor shrine suggests these souls become kind and protective spirits.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}
# As protective spirits, the {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}} guard those who remember them.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

This way, the Kibitsu Shrine uses its ties to the Kibi clan. It creates a respectful place for {{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|WD=Q2879429|lt=mizuko|1=Mizuko kuyō}}. It links them to the shrine's ancestral and protective gods.<ref name="hardacre-2023a" />{{Rp|page=239}}

== Harima Fudoki ==
The story of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}} and Inami-no-Wakiiratsume in the [[Harima Fudoki]] shows the relationship between the Kibi clan and the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}. It suggests that the {{ill|Inami, Hyōgo|en|Inami, Hyōgo|WD=Q1155194|lt=Inami|1=Inami, Hyōgo}} area was once a border between the Yamato and Kibi regions.<ref name="palmer-2015a">{{Cite book |last=Palmer |first=Edwina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWzsCgAAQBAJ&dq=%22Kibi+clan%22&pg=PA71 |title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated, and with Commentary |date=2015-11-09 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-26937-8 |language=en}}</ref>

Inami-no-Wakiiratsume, a woman from Inami, is mentioned in several ancient texts. She is thought to be the younger sister of Oiratsume from the "Kojiki,". She might have been a queen in Harima.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

The Harima Fudoki does not mention Otarashihiko/Keiko as an emperor. Instead, Otarashihiko might have been a local leader in the Inami district of the Harima coast.<ref name="palmer-2015a" />

== Kibi clan rebellion ==
{{Main|Kibi Clan Rebellion}}
The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic|1=Protohistory}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province|1=Mimasaka Province}}. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial government|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|WD=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires|1=Age of Empires (video game)}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>

The {{nihongo|Kibi Clan Rebellion|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the Kibi clan: Tasa and Oto. .<ref name="brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref><ref name="nihongi">{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William G. |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year=1896}}</ref>{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra">{{cite book |last=Kamstra |first=Jacques H. |title=Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher=Brill Archive |year=1967 |page=255}}</ref>

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|qid=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|qid=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife. The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|qid=Q695827|lt=proto-historic|1=Protohistory}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /><ref name="nihongi" />{{Sfn|Brinkley|1915|p=112}}<ref name="kamstra" />

== Decline ==

In 713, at the suggestion of ''Bizen-no-kami'' Nanten - and ''Bizen-no-suke'' Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji the  Eita, Katsuta, Tomata, Kume, Mashima, and Oba districts of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|qid=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} were separated into a new province, and, and Kamitsukeno-no-Kenji was appointed as the first governor of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|qid=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province|1=Mimasaka Province}}. This separation was the final stage of the disintegration of the former [[Kingdom of Kibi]], and was intended to further weaken the Kibi clan by putting its iron resources directly under the control of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=imperial government|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>

== In Popular culture ==
The Kibi clan features in {{ill|Age of Empires (video game)|en|Age of Empires (video game)|qid=Q220767|lt=Age of Empires|1=Age of Empires (video game)}} as an enemy faction alongside the [[Izumo clan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Russell |first=Lawrence T. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IMDmDVRF7o0C&q=%22Kibi+clan%22 |title=Age of Empires: Unauthorized Game Secrets |date=1997 |publisher=Prima Pub. |isbn=978-0-7615-1053-6 |language=en}}</ref>
